sentences.text: Stephen J.
Howard.
The J stands for James, but I don't use it.
S-T-E-P-H-E-N.
And last name is H-O-W-A-R-D.
I was born with the first name the same.
However, in Polish, we spell it differently-- S-T-E-F-A-N.
And my last name was Halpern, which was H-A-L-P-E-R-N.
In January 23, 1926.
Warsaw, Poland.
Well, Warsaw is the capital of Poland.
An it's in central Poland, practically in the middle of the country.
Well, this Stefan is-- oh, there's all kinds of diminutive names-- could be Stef, could be Stefush, could be Stefanic.
Different people called me different names, different diminutive names.
My mother called me Stef.
My aunt called me Stefush.
[LAUGHS] I don't know why.
Different people, I guess, address me differently.
And my-- our maid, who is now 80 years of age, and still lives in Poland, she still calls me Stefanic.
[LAUGHS]
Actually, my earliest memories are not from Poland.
My earliest memories are when I was four years of age, and my parents took me to different countries in Europe.
I was in France, and Germany, and Spain.
And I remember-- my first memories, actually, are from those trips.
My father was a film editor.
He spoke numerous languages.
And he translated the foreign films and put Polish subtitles into the films which appeared.
My mother spoke, in addition to Polish, she spoke Russian, and German, French, and English.
And she, too, was helping my father.
Oh, from the trip, I remember [LAUGHS] being bitten by a dog, [LAUGHS] being on the seaside, Capbreton, in-- on the Biscayne, in France.
I was in the Pyrenees Mountains, in the city named Luchon, and taking train to the top of the mountain.
I remember being in Saint Sebastian, which was in Spain.
I remember the airport in Tempelhof in Berlin, where I-- when my father arrived.
I was with my mother, and my father joined us later.
And I remember running towards him and greeting him when he arrived.
Those are kind of my first memories.
We lived, first, on a street called [?
Sosnova, ?] which was a little-- well, I would say middle-class neighborhood.
And from that area, I remember playing with some of the-- of the building's-- I guess, right now, we would call it superintendent.
My parents always felt that I should have friends.
And they were very socially minded, I guess.
And they also wanted me to play with what, at that time, were called servants, because they felt that they wanted me to have these kind of relationships with children and other people.
But the first day that I really remember well was my first day in a grammar school, where my mother took me.
And we arrived, I think, about five minutes late.
And I was alone, standing in a hall.
Didn't know where to go to.
And I was scared.
And that was my first memory from that time.
But I adjusted well to the grammar school and finished sixth grade in that school.
It was a private school.
My-- [COUGHS] my parents were not religious.
They-- frankly, the-- the first religion I learned about was Catholic religion, because our maid was Catholic.
And she would tell me about it, and even took me to church a few times to witness the mass and so on.
But of course, I knew I was Jewish, mostly because of the name, and also because I did experience anti-Semitism, even in the grammar school.
For instance, after the school, my friends would say, let's go and tease the Jews.
And I would be left alone, of course, because I wouldn't go with them.
So I felt quite alone at that time, not having friends.
Also, they would make certain remarks about Jews, and I would fight them.
And I remember my reputation was, don't tease Steve, or he's going to get wild.
[LAUGHS] I remember having fisticuffs with kids in school.
Well, they were calling names.
They were calling Jews names and making Jewish jokes.
And-- and I would get very upset and start fighting with them.
My next experience about my Jewishness was when I applied to the gymnasium.
It was a public school.
And I scored on the tests, admissions tests, very high.
And then I found out that I wasn't admitted.
And somebody told me very frankly that I wasn't admitted because they had a quota for Jewish students.
And out of the class of several hundred, I think they accepted five Jewish kids into that public school.
So that my parents had to pay for a private gymnasium.
No.
No.
My mother was.
She-- I didn't really quite understand what was going on, but I knew that she was very concerned.
She was very concerned.
And she always seemed to worry about me and worry about our future, my future, in Poland.
Well, I attended one year of gymnasium.
I'm sorry.
And I was about to start the second year, when the war started, September 1, 1939.

And it was kind of a-- well, I don't want to say funny, but in a way, it was funny, because a few days before the war started, my mother persuaded my father-- my parents were divorced, by the way, in 1936, or 1935.
My father remarried in 1936.
I lived with my father and stepmother.
But my mother, in 1939, persuaded my father to place me in a suburb of Warsaw with friends of theirs, because she suspected that there would be war.
Well, the first bombs that fell were on that place [LAUGHS] where I was in the suburbs.
So they told me to take a taxi and go back to Warsaw.
So I went back to Warsaw, and I spent the Polish war in Warsaw.
Well, during the Polish war-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it was a very difficult time, because Warsaw was declared an open city by the Germans and was bombarded day and night with artillery and planes.
And there were very, very close calls during that time-- 25 days.
I was part of the time with my father, and part of the time with my mother.
And we were very, very lucky, because there were many people killed around us.
And we were lucky we survived.
At that time, we lived on [NON-ENGLISH], Ninth Street, which was a upper, middle-class neighborhood, next to the American embassy.
And a bomb fell on a gas station which was between the American embassy and our home, nearly destroying the apartment building.
So at that time, my father decided that maybe the Germans were trying to bomb the American embassy.
So he sent me to my mother.
And I spent the rest of the war days with my mother in her apartment.
We stayed, most of the time, in the basement.
We didn't have any food.
I was eating Jello, [LAUGHS] which was in the form of hard-- I don't know how to call it.
The kind of Jello that they had at that time, you had to put in the hot water and boil the hot water to make the Jello.
Well, I was taking an ax and cutting these little pieces of the Jello and eating it.
That was about my-- my food for about at least a week.
And we survived.
The 25th of September was the worst night, in which the air bombardment was 12 hours from morning to evening.
And the Warsaw was ablaze.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
And then, the Polish Army gave up.
Surrendered.
And the Germans came in.
Well, at that time, I-- that was the day I went back to my father because after the bombardment, I wanted to see whether he survived.
And so I left there.
And he was fine.
Yeah.
I went to-- that-- that was on a Monday, Monday, the 25th of September.
And I went back to my father in the evening.
And he was fine.
So I stayed there.
And the next day, Tuesday, we suddenly saw the German planes flying over, very low.
And we couldn't understand why.
And then, the radio told us that Warsaw had surrendered.
So [CLEARS THROAT] then, [DRINKS WATER] we continued living at-- I continued living with my father and my stepmother.
There was an-- [CLEARS THROAT] may I tell you a very interesting incident, though?
Yes.
Our-- our apartment was on the third floor, which was the upper floor of that apartment building.
And it was still during the war, during the 25 days that Warsaw was surrounded.
And there was a doorbell.
And I opened the door, and a mass of people practically trampled me over, shrieking, Gas!
Gas!
The civil defense people gave us little masks made of cotton with a little fluid on it.
And when it was a gas attack, we were supposed to put it on our face.
So the people came on that third floor, because gas is supposed to be heavy, going down.
So the higher you go, the chances of survival.
So the apartment was filled with about 30 people running around.
And I took out my mask and put it on my face.
I was 13 at that time.
And then a man suddenly came to me, ripped the mask off my face, and put it on himself.
[LAUGHS] That was my experience.
And of course, there was no gas.
This was all a false alarm.
But I remember that incident.
OK.
My father, as I told you, was a film editor.
And he did much of his work at home.
And we had a table at home, on which he was editing the films.
You put earphones on you, and you look into a-- a little screen, and you-- you run the film.
And he put these signs in it.
Well, anyhow, the Germans immediately declared, of course, that the Jews were not supposed to get any work.
So the studios in Warsaw refused to give my father the job.
We had to dismantle the table.
Well, first, some people in the studio felt very, of course, supportive to us.
So I would sneak out to the-- to the studio, and I would pick up the reels of-- the metal reels with film.
And I would pack them so they wouldn't be visible.
And I took them to my father.
And he still would work on the films.
But it became very dangerous, because people were coming to the apartment.
And the Gestapo was coming in.
So we had to disassemble the table, took out all the thing, and covered it with a plywood, and put things on it, so it wouldn't be seen that it was a table.
Well one day, a number of Germans-- and I don't even think they were Gestapo.
I think they were just Wehrmacht, the Army, maybe there was Gestapo, SS-- just came into the apartment and started rummaging around.
And after a while, they just left.
And then, after that, we found out there was a number of things missing, like crystal.
They came in with big coats, with big pockets.
And apparently, whenever they saw a crystal vase or something, they just put it into their pocket.
So that was another of my early experiences with the Germans, at that time.
The next memory was the-- well, the declaration that ghetto was being established, and all the Jews had to move to the ghetto.
My mother, who did not look Jewish, didn't want to go to the ghetto.
But my father was much older.
He didn't want to hide.
He felt he could not do that.
And he said, no, we have to go to the ghetto.
And since I lived with my father, my mother was not able to support me.
My mother decided to move into the ghetto with us.
The day we went, my mother took a-- what do you call those?
Like in Central Park, in New York, with the horse drawn carriage, you know, they have New York park?
Well, Warsaw was full of those.
Those were the taxis in Warsaw.
So we took one of them and put our belongings in it.
And this horse driven carriage-- we went through the gate into the ghetto.
And that was memorable day, entering the ghetto, and where I'm going to spend next two years in.
And again, my mother had another apartment in the ghetto.
And I was with my father and stepmother.
We-- our apartment was opposite a bakery.
And they decided they had to, of course, to support themselves in some ways.
My stepmother was a very cook-- good cook and baker.
And we hired an assistant baker.
And she started baking rolls, and cakes, and cookies, and so on.
And I would go and sell them in the ghetto.
I would go around to different apartments and sell them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I will-- I guess I'm going to lose-- lose some of the German words.
They had razzias.
Razzia is when the German Gestapo would come in and surround an area and took the Jews away to a labor camp, or whatever.
Well some of the-- unfortunately, some of the Jews in the ghetto were taking advantage of it.
And they were, sometimes, panicking people by going down the street and whispering to people, razzia, razzia.
And we would then hide in the apartment buildings.
But they only wanted the people to go in the apartment building so they could steal whatever they were carrying.
So one day, one of those people came in, told me razzia.
I went to a door and hide-- and hid there.
And he took all my rolls and cakes and just run away with them.
And that was a lesson which I learned.
And never again had I allowed this to happen.
A somewhat traumatic event I remember also was when it was raining.
And the Germans were using a number of the Jewish factories in the ghetto to make uniforms for the soldiers.
And one day, I was walking down what was known as Leshno Street, one of the main streets.
And a big German truck drove by and stopped in front of the factory.
And they started unloading bales of material for the uniforms.
And one of the Germans caught me and put this big bale on my [NON-ENGLISH] on my shoulder, and asked me to carry it to the factory, which was about, oh, I would say, at least 150 feet away.
It was raining.
There were puddles of water.
He took out a gun, and he told me [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [DRINKS WATER] that-- [EMOTIONAL] [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] he told me that, if I was going to drop the bale, he was going to shoot me.
And as I was carrying this thing, I really thought that I was going to drop it.
I just-- it was too heavy for me, but I somehow made it.
[EMOTIONAL] [SIGHS] Another time, my mother, of course, who was very much involved with art, theater, and so on-- there was a Jewish theater in the ghetto.
And she took me to a play, to see a play with famous Jewish actors.
[COUGHS] In the middle of the play, the Germans surrounded the theater and took everybody in the courtyard, put them against the wall.
We were all against the wall.
They took the guns and pointed at us and started shooting.
And they shoot right above our heads.
And then they laughed.
[EMOTIONAL] My mother also always impressed me the importance of learning, education.
And she has found number of teachers there, professors, university professors.
And she has organized a underground school.
And we would meet in different apartments, each day in a different apartment, so we wouldn't be found out.
And there were about, oh, I would say, eight of us.
And we would be having normal-- [SCOFFS] normal-- regular school subjects.
I remember my first lesson in trigonometry [CHUCKLES] and algebra.
I remember, I was very good in trigonometry and helping other students.
And this was after my round selling the rolls and cakes in the evening-- afternoon.
Well, it couldn't be in the evening, because we had a 8 o'clock, you know, curfew.
We had to be home.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
What other experiences do I remember?
I remember the typhus epidemic.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] People were dying, some of them lying on the street.
At first, I remember we used to see that person on the sidewalk, we would cross the street and go on the other side.
And then, after a while, we just stepped over them.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
And the-- the typhus was carried by lice.
And this one lesson, which we all learned, we never would sit on a chair with a cushion in it, because lice would be inside of the cushion.
We only could sit on a hard wooden chair.
Any time on the street, if you brushed against it, you know, walking by, you would immediately look at your sleeve to see if there were any lice on it.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] You heard shootings every night. 8 o'clock-- sometimes, I had to go to a store to buy supplies for the bakery for next day.
And there was a long line of people in the store.
Or sometimes, 8 o'clock came, I was still in the store.
They would close the front, AND then we would just sneak out and run home, hoping that we would not see any Germans who would shoot on sight.
Well, that was-- oh, hm, another thing's come to my mind.
My stepmother, one day, remembered suddenly that it was the birthday of her best friend, who lived just a few blocks away.
She quickly baked one of her little cakes, the size of my palm of my hand, put white paper on it and a string.
And she asked me to go and deliver it to her friend, who was just a few blocks away.
And she said to me, be careful that nobody steals it from you.
And I said-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I said, no way.
I'm going to put my hand between the paper and the string, the string on the outside, so they couldn't take it away from me.
Well, I was very naive.
I was carrying it on the street when, suddenly, from nowhere, this man came and grabbed the parcel out of my hand.
And then, realizing that he couldn't get it because my head was attached to it, he just bit into this on my hand and ate the cake from my hand.
[DRINKS WATER] [SIGHS] This is the sort of thing-- something for Schindler's film.
[EMOTIONAL] [LAUGHS]
There were stores.
There were stores there.
I mean, we were all hungry, but it was not enough to live, too much to die.
There were stores selling sugar, and flour, and things like this.
Well, the money from the selling of the-- of the bread, and rolls, and the cookies.
Oh, they just left.
They laughed and left.
And we were-- this was a shattering experience.
But they just-- they just left.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] In old cities, the apartment buildings are built in such a way that there's like a little courtyard in the middle.
And the buildings all around the courtyard, the apartment building.
I don't know if I can tell this story.
